title: Monday
html:
The bathroom biofilm registered overnight.

---

[External input detected. Origin: unclassified. Processing.]

[New organism variants: 4 additional variants identified. Classification: extremophile bacteria, iron-oxidizing bacteria, sulfur-reducing bacteria, biofilm matrix organisms.]

[Note: Extremophile variants show elevated tolerance to temperature extremes. Survival probability in current planetary conditions: 61%.]

---

61%. That's the highest survival probability any organism I've sent has had. The single-cell organisms from the Shop were rated at 40% and most of them died anyway. The iron-oxidizing bacteria from my bathroom grout have a better shot at surviving minus 36 degrees than anything I paid for.

I check the full panel.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Developing]

[Atmospheric development: 56% complete.]

[Temperature range: -34 to -40 degrees Celsius.]

[Purchased organisms (x40): 6 surviving. Stable.]

[Unclassified organisms: 15 variants active. Population: estimated 14,700.]

[Rudimentary flora: 2 established, 1 marginal. Photosynthetic activity stable. Atmospheric contribution: low.]

[Life Energy generated: 3.91]

---

3.91. Up from 3.18 yesterday. The biofilm variants are already reproducing.

A third plant is marginal, which means it's alive but not established. I only sent 4 stems. If that third one stabilizes I'll have 3 contributing to the atmosphere. The more atmosphere, the warmer it gets. The warmer it gets, the faster everything else grows.

I close the panel and make rice.

It's Sunday. The property company answer comes tomorrow. Rent is due tomorrow. Both of those things land on the same day, which is not a coincidence I arranged but is also not something I can do anything about. I pay rent regardless. The answer from Mr. Sorin is yes or no and I can't affect it by thinking about it.

I eat the rice and open my laptop.

The insurance company rejection email arrived overnight. Polite, generic, thanked me for my time. I move it to the rejection folder. The folder has eight entries now.

I spend the morning on applications. Three new postings, two follow-up emails on applications that are old enough to follow up on. The follow-up email format I've settled on is four sentences: name, position applied for, date, one line about still being interested. I don't know if it changes outcomes. It's something to send.

By noon I've done everything I can do on the job front until someone replies.

I think about the planet.

The biofilm worked better than expected, which means the selection logic is different from what I assumed. I assumed complexity mattered most. The extremophiles aren't more complex than the soil bacteria, they're specialists. They evolved for hostile conditions. Minus 40 degrees is hostile. The planet selects for things that can handle it, which means I should be sending things that evolved for cold, not things that are generally robust.

I think about what's in this building that evolved for cold. Nothing, probably. This building was not designed for cold. But I have a motorbike and the city has other environments.

I look at the map application on my phone. The river runs through the northern part of the city. Rivers have sediment, and riverbank sediment at the waterline has organisms that handle temperature swings, floods, drying out. That's not the same as minus 40 but it's more resilient than whatever was in the bathroom.

The market district has drains. Drains accumulate things from multiple sources, concentrated in one place.

I make a list of collection sites.

Then I look at the clock. 1 PM Sunday. The river is 20 minutes by motorbike. I have nothing else to do today and I've already eaten.

I get the takeaway containers.

***

The riverbank is lower than I expected, a concrete slope down to a narrow strip of mud and stones at the waterline. There are two people fishing further along and one woman walking a dog. None of them look at me when I crouch at the waterline and start filling a container with mud.

The mud is dark, almost black, and it smells of river, organic and faintly sulfuric. That sulfur is probably something living. I fill two containers. I take a water sample in a third, river water with whatever's suspended in it. I pull up a clump of the stringy green growth from the rocks at the waterline, algae or something close to it, and put it in the fourth container.

On the way back I stop at the market drains. The gutter near the wet section of the market, where the fish stalls are, has a buildup of organic matter in the corners where the drain cover sits. I scrape some of it into a clean container. It's not a good smell. I take it anyway.

***

Back in my room I press everything against the panel in sequence. Riverbank mud first, then river water, then the algae clump, then the market drain material.

---

[External input detected. Origin: unclassified. Processing.]

[Riverbank sediment: 9 organism variants identified. Includes: iron-reducing bacteria, methane-producing archaea, diatoms, rotifer eggs (dormant), nematode population, fungal spores.]

[River water: 3 additional variants. Includes: suspended algae, cyanobacteria.]

[Algae sample: Classification — filamentous algae. Multicellular. Viability assessment: 34% in current conditions, rising with atmospheric development.]

[Market drain organic: 6 variants identified. Includes: decomposer bacteria, cellulose-degrading fungi, mold spores.]

[Total new variants this input: 18.]

[Rotifer eggs detected. Note: Dormant state allows survival in extreme conditions. Activation expected upon atmospheric development reaching 65%.]

---

Rotifer eggs. I look that up. Rotifers are microscopic animals, technically, not plants or bacteria. The simplest animals. They eat algae and bacteria, they reproduce fast, and some species can survive desiccation and extreme cold by going dormant. The system flagged dormant eggs, which means they're not reproducing yet, but they will if the atmosphere develops enough.

Animals on the planet. I didn't buy any animals. I accidentally carried them in river mud.

---

[Life Energy generated: 4.44]

---

It jumped 0.53 while I was reading about rotifers.

I close the panel and sit for a while.

The pattern is becoming clearer. The Shop sells organisms calibrated for a standard progression: atmosphere first, then simple life, then complex life in sequence. What I've been doing is sending Earth's full ecosystem complexity all at once and letting the planet sort out what survives. Most of it dies. The things that don't are the things already adapted to harsh conditions. And harsh-condition specialists generate Life Energy at roughly the same rate as the Shop's mid-tier organisms, at a fraction of the cost, which is zero.

The problem is I'm still generating 4 Life Energy per day. I need 11 for this to cover rent. Rotifers and cyanobacteria are not going to close that gap fast.

I check the time. 4 PM Sunday. Rent is due tomorrow morning. The savings account has one month covered, which I've already accepted. The property company answer is also tomorrow.

I open the Authority and navigate to a tab I haven't checked carefully before: Conversion.

---

[Life Energy Conversion]

[Current accumulated Life Energy: 41.7]

[Note: Accumulated Life Energy reflects total generated since registration, minus purchases.]

[Conversion to home currency: Available at any accumulated amount above 10 Life Energy.]

[Minimum conversion: 10 Life Energy.]

[At current conversion rate: 10 Life Energy = approximately 1/100th of median monthly rent.]

---

41.7 accumulated. Conversion is available. 10 Life Energy equals about one percent of a month's rent.

I do the full math. 41.7 Life Energy, converted, is about 4% of a month's rent. Enough to cover a few days of food. Not enough to matter for rent, which is due in full.

But it's there. It's real money I can convert. I haven't touched the conversion function because I assumed I needed to hit 1,000 first. The minimum is 10.

I convert 10 Life Energy and watch what happens.

---

[Conversion confirmed. 10 Life Energy deducted. Equivalent amount deposited to registered account. Processing time: up to 24 hours.]

---

Registered account. I didn't register an account. I didn't know there was a registration step.

I go to Help and look it up.

---

[Account Registration: Constellation Managers may register a home currency account for Life Energy conversions. Registration requires account details to be submitted through the Authority's account settings.]

---

I navigate to account settings. There's a field for bank account details. I fill it in. Then I go back to Conversion and look at the 10 Life Energy I already converted before I registered the account.

---

[Pending conversion: 10 Life Energy. Account registration required to complete transfer. Please complete account registration to proceed.]

---

The conversion is pending. It's waiting for the account I just registered. Processing time: up to 24 hours. Rent is due in the morning.

I stare at that for a moment. 10 Life Energy is not enough to matter. The pending conversion is not going to cover rent. The account registration will probably process tonight but the money won't arrive before morning.

I close the Authority and check my savings account. The number is the same as this morning. I transfer rent to the landlord, which takes the number down to the three-week food and transport amount. The rent is paid.

I open a new tab and look at job listings for twenty minutes without applying to anything. Then I close the laptop and check the planet.

---

[Rotifer eggs: Dormant. Atmospheric threshold for activation: 65%.]

[Filamentous algae: 1 of 3 specimens establishing root adhesion on rocky substrate.]

[Unclassified organisms: 29 variants active. Population: estimated 31,000.]

[Life Energy generated: 5.12]

---

31,000. It was 14,700 this morning.

5.12 Life Energy per day. The jump from the river mud is already showing. 29 variants.

I look at the rotifer note again. 65% atmosphere for activation. Current atmosphere is 56%. If the plants and cyanobacteria keep contributing, 65% is maybe 3 or 4 days away.

When the rotifers activate, they'll start eating the bacteria and algae. That's a predator-prey cycle. The first real ecological loop on the planet.

I've been thinking about this wrong. I've been trying to maximize organism count because count drives Life Energy. But what drives count is ecological complexity. Rotifers eating bacteria means bacteria reproduce faster to compensate, which is what prey populations do. More bacteria, more rotifers, upward pressure on both numbers.

I make a note to come back to the river.

The phone doesn't ring. No email arrives. I check my application list and update the status columns. Tomorrow there's a property company answer, a pending conversion, and 31,000 organisms on a planet I haven't named yet.

I haven't thought about naming it. The system calls it Sphere 7-A.

I look at that for a second. Sphere 7-A is accurate. It's a sphere. It's designated 7-A. It's also got 31,000 organisms on it now, two plants doing photosynthesis, dormant rotifer eggs waiting for 9 more percentage points of atmosphere, and iron-oxidizing bacteria from my bathroom grout colonizing the rocky substrate.

Sphere 7-A is fine.

I close everything and go to sleep.

***

Monday.

The email from the property management company arrives at 8:14 AM. I read it before I'm fully awake.

Mr. Sorin thanks me for coming in. He says the team discussed the position and they've decided to go with a candidate whose background is a closer fit for their current needs. He wishes me success in my search.

I put the phone face-down on the bed and look at the ceiling for a while.

That's nine rejections. Thirty-one applications, nine counted, the rest silence. The property company was the most promising one. Mr. Sorin asked the right questions and the answer was still no.

The savings account is at three weeks of food and transport. No income. No interview scheduled. The planet is generating 5 Life Energy per day, needs to be generating 11, and I've converted 10 Life Energy to an account that hasn't received the deposit yet.

I check the Authority.

---

[Pending conversion: Completed. Transfer processed.]

[Account registered. Future conversions will process within 2 hours.]

---

I check my bank account. The deposit is there. A small amount, less than a meal at a sit-down restaurant, but it arrived.

10 Life Energy converted to actual money. The system works. The conversion tab works. The rate is bad and the amount is small and it arrived.

I sit on the edge of the bed and look at the number in my bank account for a moment longer than necessary.

Then I open the planet panel.

---

[Planet designation: Sphere 7-A]

[Status: Developing]

[Atmospheric development: 61% complete.]

[Temperature range: -30 to -36 degrees Celsius.]

[Unclassified organisms: 29 variants active. Population: estimated 47,000.]

[Rudimentary flora: 3 established. Photosynthetic activity: increasing.]

[Life Energy generated: 6.83]

---

6.83.

The third plant established overnight. 47,000 organisms. Atmosphere at 61%, which is past the 60% threshold where the weeds had a 41% survival chance, which means the two that are already established are now more stable than they were. 65% for the rotifers is 4 percentage points away.

6.83 per day. Up from 5.12 yesterday. Still needs to be 11.

I get up and make rice. I open my laptop and look at the job listings. I apply to two positions before the rice is ready. I eat standing at the counter and apply to a third.

The application list is at 34 entries. 9 rejections counted. The others are silence.

I need to go back to the river.
